data.title: SHLD: The Defense ETF That's Crushing Big Tech
data.description:
While I remain on largely bullish on big tech the frothy valuations of some of the companies concern me. This is why one SHLD has caught my attention due to it both crushing indices like QQQ over the past three years while also having exposure to the defense industry, one which has a robust growth profile. Since researching SHLD I have since bought shares and have concluded that it deserves a Strong Buy rating.

This article will analyze SHLD against its peers and explain why it's in such a strong position moving forward.

Overview

The Global X Defense Tech ETF (NYSEARCA:SHLD) aims to mirror the performance and the yield of the Global X Defense Tech Index. SHLD invests in companies that are considered leaders in cyber security, AI, hardware, and military systems. The ETF was incepted in September 2023 and has $3.5 billion of assets under management with an expense ratio of 0.50%.

The ETF has high concentration risk as the top ten holdings comprises 62% of its total value, and presently only has 45 holdings.

Some of the largest holdings include Palantir, RTX Corporation, Rheinmetall AG, and Lockheed Martin.

SHLD is passively managed, meaning that the manager does not try to beat the index nor make any short-term technical plays or shifts. It's a physical replication model meaning that it purchases actual shares to replicate the index.

For a company to be included in the index, more than 50% of its revenue must come from defense-tech and adjacent industries. This includes industries such as cybersecurity, Internet of Things (IOT), and advanced military systems.

The index is free-float and market-cap weighted. This means that a company cannot comprise more than 8% of the index's total value. There are also volume, regional, and liquidity requirements that firms must meet in order to be included.

Valuation

SHLD's value has increased 153% over the past three years, and grew 68.12% year-to-date alone. Investors might worry that its rally might be overheated, but analyst forecasts for the top nine of its holdings show promising median revenue, EPS, and share price increases.

For the top nine holdings, the group is expected to grow its EPS by an average of 12.59%, while revenue is expected to grow 5.27%. PLTR is the notable outlier among its peers. Although its absolute weighting is capped at 8%, it's driving a significant percentage of the average revenue and EPS increases for the group. If one already feels bullish on PLTR, then the bull case becomes stronger for SHLD and vice-versa.

In sum, with accretive revenue, EPS, and share price increases already forecasted for SHLD, it lays the groundwork for a continued rally.

Comparing SHLD to its peers paints an even clearer picture that it's in a strong position to continue to surge higher. On a total return basis, SHLD returned 156%, far exceeding the returns of my constructed peer group that includes ITA, XAR, PPA, and NATO.

SHLD has a 1-year expense-adjusted return of 71.6% which is far ahead of its peer group. The reason for its core outperformance comes from a sharper tilt toward defense-tech names such as Palantir. Liquidity is robust at $67.8M daily turnover and its expense ratio of 0.50% is the highest among its peers, but still competitive for a thematic ETF.

SHLD then is the most competitive option for investors when compared with its peer group of thematically-similar ETFs.

It may also be a viable alternative to pure tech exposure if one is seeking growth and wants to avoid the rich multiples found in the Majestic 7, and it has outperformed QQQ by over 99% in return over the past three years.

Why Invest in SHLD now?

I see several durable catalysts as reasons for investors to buy shares of SHLD. The first comes from broader tailwinds in the defense industry. NATO has the expectation that all of its allies should meet or exceed 2% of its GDP defense in 2025, and some leaders in European nations have endorsed a 3.5% target for core defense spending.

Meanwhile, spending in the U.S is both enormous and heavily tilted towards defense-tech, an area that SHLD has carved out a thematic niche for itself. $849.9 billion has been authorized for Department of Defense spending. Of that, $143.8B has been earmarked specifically for spending in AI, cyber defense, and advanced systems, which fits exactly into SHLD's wheelhouse.

Some notable wins for SHLD's main holdings include Rheinmetall projecting 80 billion euros in new orders by mid 2026. L3Harris is securing multi-year satellite and communications contracts, and Palantir is embedding AI into defense procurement systems.

SHLD's unique positioning with a tilt into defense tech then is a huge advantage, especially as AI and other long-term tech super cycles dovetail with military spending. I think there's little chance that this convergence will weaken and most likely will accelerate due to growing geopolitical tensions.

Risks

One of the main risks is in SHLD is the reality that volatility works both ways: It has been able to beat even pure tech with high-beta names but that too may lead to sharp drawdowns if the market regime changes. This risk is amplified due to SHLD's construction, with 62% of its assets sitting in just ten of its core holdings.

SHLD can be exposed to strong price swings and regime changes in both defense and tech. With defense/industrials also being cyclical industries, risks remain elevated. However, I do not see these risks as near-term headwinds. ETFs such as ITA and PPA, by contrast offer steadier and potentially less volatile exposure, but on a risk-adjusted basis SHLD has offered a better deal per unit of risk invested over the last three years.

Takeaway

SHLD is one of the best picks for investors looking for exposure to the defense industry and its constituents all expect to continue growing their EPS and top lines going into next year. Although its expense ratio is substantial, it has managed to beat QQQ over the past three years, and the current macro set up to me favors continued outperformance. I then reiterate my Strong Buy rating for SHLD and have initiated a long position.

data.title: Strategy: Leveraged Bitcoin Exposure, Without The ETF Ceiling
data.description:
Strategy Inc. (NASDAQ:MSTR) is an interesting, complex investment opportunity as the world's biggest Bitcoin treasury company. The company fully embraced this development when it rebranded from MicroStrategy in February 2025. At that time, Strategy made the complete transformative change to a leveraged Bitcoin proxy company, underpinned with a secondary enterprise software business and has been building its holdings ever since to a total of 628,946 bitcoins, worth approximately $75+ billion. Several analysts, including Standard Chartered and CoinShares, project that Bitcoin will reach between $150,000 and $200,000 within the next 12–18 months. With Strategy trading at a relatively modest 1.84x Bitcoin NAV multiple, it has asymmetric upside potential weighed against considerable dilution (with 2 million 'potential' shares upon completion of their debt exchange) and refinancing risk (as bondholders want to be converted to equity). We recommend a HOLD rating due to the significant upside potential if Bitcoin hits analyst targets, balanced by substantial dilution risk, refinancing uncertainty, and 2.5x volatility compared to Bitcoin itself. Investors should size positions accordingly, typically between 3-5% of their portfolios

Investment Thesis

Strategy is the largest corporate Bitcoin treasury company in the world, not the "one and only" as some claim. Other companies such as Tesla (11,509 BTC), Marathon Digital (50,639 BTC), and Coinbase (11,776 BTC) also maintain significant Bitcoin holdings, though their business models are distinct and they are not direct competitors to Strategy; however, if we just look at Strategy alone, it has 628,946 bitcoins, which is roughly 3% of Bitcoin’s total supply of approximately 19.91 million BTC as of August 2025, according to CoinMetrics data and more than all other corporate treasuries combined. Due to its size advantage, with its comparative low cost basis and sophisticated execution in capital markets, Strategy can offer institutional investors seeking bitcoin exposure a unique differentiated value proposition.

The company's strategic transformation is now mathematically complete. While Strategy has a functioning software business generating $114.5 million in revenue as a software product on a quarterly basis, this revenue is broken down plainly in the complete context of the company and in monetized terms (i.e., mere 0.15% of the overall $75 billion plus in Bitcoin treasury value). Thus, it is clear the software business helps provide operational continuity, but it does not generate enterprise value whatsoever - certainly almost all of the market cap of Strategy comes from Bitcoin and not operational performance.

No doubt, Bitcoin price movements in line with nearly all analysts targets in the $150,000-$200,000 range create unbelievably significant upside opportunity for Strategy shareholders. The management's invented "BTC Yield" metric generating, 25% YTD 2025, gives you a systematic way to accumulate Bitcoin per share, that will yield more productive results than simply Bitcoin ownership, if it is managed correctly. That being said, this yield/reward comes at a massive level of equity dilution, which will need to be profiled analytically and monitored.

About The Company And Its Value Creation

Strategy operates under a complex dual-pillar business model, but now the company has begun to operate in a nearly entirely Bitcoin treasury-focused manner, which represents an evolution of enterprise value creation from several historical business lines.

The first pillar is Strategy's Bitcoin treasury, currently holding at an average cost basis of $73,288, for a total cost basis investment of $46.1 billion to date. This Bitcoin treasury is creating unrealized appreciation and is essentially flowing through the company's balance sheet using fair value accounting to quarterly earnings at market prices approaching $120,000 per Bitcoin price. A great example is that Strategy's Q2 2025 operating income of $14.03 billion derived all from appreciation of Bitcoin, showcasing the complete absurdity of how treasury operations now value in comparison to other business operations by several orders of magnitude.

Strategy’s bitcoin position gives the company scale advantages that smaller treasury operators cannot attain. Strategy's systematic accumulation program, what our management team refers to as a "Bitcoin reactor," continually transforms premium equity and avant-garde preferred securities into more Bitcoin by measured execution in capital markets.

The second pillar consists of Strategy's traditional enterprise software business for business intelligence and analytics, which includes Strategy One and Strategy Mosaic. These cloud-native solutions serve the retail, banking, healthcare, and public sectors, providing scalable solutions with strategic partnerships including AWS, Microsoft Azure, and Google Cloud. The Q2 2025 software revenue of $114.5 million achieved modestly year-over-year growth of 2%, however subscription revenue increased substantially at 70% growth.

Nonetheless, the contribution of the software segment to Strategy’s total enterprise value is negligible. Despite unequaled 68.8% gross margin and accelerated subscription growth, the quarterly software revenue is still less than 0.2% of total enterprise value. This mathematical reality negates all ideas of operational diversification—Strategy's investment thesis exists solely because of the appreciation of Bitcoin and its ability to accumulate fractionally over time.

Value creation occurs through the proprietary "NYSEARCA:BTC yield" measure created by Strategy; which offers a reflection of annualized bitcoin accumulation per share. Year-to-date 2025, the company achieved a 25% BTC yield which is dramatically better than the originally proposed targets of less than 10%, and management has increased full year guidance to 30% because of the exceptionally strong performance. For current investors, this works out to 198,543 satoshis per share, which is considerably better than 158,827 satoshis per share during 2024. The BTC yield is created by issuing equity and preferreds at premiums to Bitcoin net asset value so that fractional purchases of Bitcoin can occur higher percentage-wise, than shares are diluted whenever market conditions permit a reasonable execution.

Competition And Competitive Position

Strategy is certainly not the only company in the ever-expanding Bitcoin treasury ecosystem since it is the leader with a growing field of over 145 companies holding it on the balance sheet, but Strategy is able to extend competition with size-based advantages that smaller competitors cannot replicate.

If investors are looking for direct Bitcoin exposure without the risk of leverage, credit, or management, then they can find less risky ways to obtain direct exposure to Bitcoin through spot exchange-traded funds (ETFs). Blackrock's IBIT and Fidelity's FBTC offer direct Bitcoin exposure with minimal expense ratios of roughly 0.25% and without concern of premiums or discounts. The ETFs are superior to Strategy as they minimize outright dilution risk, eliminate any stress on convertible debt and management, but they cannot systematically accumulate Bitcoin and clearly they cannot offer any superior returns on Bitcoin if the market has been favorable.

Strategy's differential building competitive advantages come from three unique capabilities that competitors cannot easily replicate at scale. First and foremost, the company holds first-mover credibility because it is the first Bitcoin treasury company providing institutional credibility and brand awareness throughout the cryptocurrency ecosystem. Second, the company has sophisticated capital markets access, which gives Strategy the ability to systematically acquire bitcoins using zero-coupon convertible bonds, preferred securities (revolutionary), and disciplined equity issuances—all unavailable to other corporate treasuries. Third, the visionary leadership of Michael Saylor has built a substantial brand premium and institutional following that results in favorable financing terms and significant institutional investor demand.

The competition for enterprise software has become less and less relevant to Strategy's investment thesis. The company competes with large competitors, including Salesforce's Tableau, Microsoft Power BI, and Qlik, in business intelligence markets.

Financial Analysis

The financial performance of Strategy represents its evolution as a Bitcoin investment vehicle, and traditional equity valuation metrics will prove to be more of a distraction than an effective analysis. The company's Q2 25 results revealed the kind of math that it is indeed. It is revealing extraordinary earnings based solely on Bitcoin price appreciation and not from operational improvement.

Seeking Alpha's Quant rating system produces complex accounting while simply retaining a HOLD rating, features widespread market opportunity, while simultaneously acknowledging the inherent risks of Strategy's Bitcoin-leveraged business.

Almost comically, the forward P/E ratio of 8.9x is being offered at a 70% discount to sector multiples based on an average of 29.7x. As a company entirely reliant on earnings derived from Bitcoin implied fair value, the forward P/E ratio is misplaced. Statistically, the forward EV/EBITDA of 8.6x are equally compelling to report at a 55% discount to sector average multiples were indeed distorted by Bitcoin's treasury impact rather than traditional operating fundamentals.

More important is Strategy's reported net margin of 1,037% versus the sector's 4.28%. The net margin reflects the direct transfer of Bitcoin capital gains to the income statement utilizing a fair value accounting method. This phenomenal margin illustrates the mathematical effects of Strategy's leveraged exposure to Bitcoin during cryptocurrency price appreciation while emphasizing the potential for extreme earnings volatility during periods of adverse price movements in Bitcoin.

The company's diluted earnings per share of $32.60 and $10.02 billion net income is a product of fair value accounting gains on Bitcoin holdings. The company's management provided FY2025 guidance of $80 diluted earnings per share assuming Bitcoin reaches $150,000, further illustrating either binary earnings sensitivity to cryptocurrency price moves rather than operational performance.

The valuation framework most relevant for Strategy is its multiple compared to Bitcoin net asset value (mNAV) of 1.84x, within a historical range of 1.5x-4.0x. This premium reflects investors' view of the value embedded in professional treasury management, systematic accumulation capabilities, and access to capital markets that are not available via direct Bitcoin ownership. At current multiples, Strategy investors are exposed to roughly 0.002 Bitcoin per share or 198,543 satoshis, as tangible Bitcoin accumulation above and beyond what is possible in simply purchasing cryptocurrency.

The balance sheet is strong despite concentration and its mix of cryptocurrencies. Strategy maintains a conservative debt-to-equity of 0.109 with $8.2 billion in convertible debt collateralized by Bitcoin worth $75.5 billion. With this approach comes refinancing risk with respect to the convertible debt if Bitcoin trades below strike prices (which range between $307-$433) at maturity of the convertible debt between 2028-2030. With cash liquidity of $50.1 million, they won't have much cushion for operations, which appropriately reflects that management has effectively gambled on Bitcoin as its primary treasury asset.

The debt structure greatly features asymmetric risk characteristics through staggered maturity zero-coupon convertible bonds. The funding creates value for shareholders through embedded call option value with conversion only occurring when share prices exceed their strike prices of between $307 - $433. The rights structure provides attractive upside participation, while limiting downside participation. They have the opportunity to engage equity-like behaviour with an attractive downside without unnecessary equity funding during a period of favorable terms for inducing costs of capital.

By applying conservative multiples of 2.2x to mNAV (historically in line with averages) and taking conservative assumptions on a Bitcoin price of $150,000 rooted in multiple analysts forecasts, Strategy's intrinsic value is moving to $480 a share providing potential upside of approximately 110% upside from current levels. These projections depend on continued BTC Yield generation and approximately a modest contribution from the software business, but are considerably dependent on Bitcoin price action strategy and how management allocated capital historically.

Technical Analysis

On the mensual chart, we see that since the double-bottom formation at the 3.26 support level (valid since 2017 and only broken briefly in 2020) in mid-2022, the price has risen quickly inside a bullish channel from 3.26 to $543, which is an appreciation of more than 3,985% in almost two years. At the end of 2024, the price tested an end-of-year high, breaking up from the channel but leaving a long wick on the candle and experiencing a spike in volume. After that, the price was retraced to the prior highs around 220 dollars; after this retrace, the price went on to retest the high levels.

The current level is significant resistance, and therefore, difficult to overcome initially (in the shortest term). The most likely movement will be a pullback to the 50 sessions mean, or possibly to the bottom of the channel, which coincidentally overlaps the 100 session exponential average.

About the stochastic, it has recently come into a more neutral area, which will allow for the potential of a new bullish impulse. However, with the technical context as it is most likely the scenario will be a pullback to a support zone before trying to attempt a break of the ATH.

The correlation analysis has shown MSTR has a correlation of 0.65 with Bitcoin over a 12-month period but with approximately 2.5 times the volatility. This enhancement of the movements is to be expected with a leveraged proxy, which carries both higher opportunity and risk. The break of key resistance at 450 and 543 would allow technical targets with a clear link to fundamentals.

Catalysts

There are several constructive structural catalysts for strategy that should lead to substantial upside appreciation over the next 12-18 months based on execution and market circumstances. Bitcoin price appreciation is the primary structural catalyst; management's price target of $150,000 for Bitcoin would imply $34 billion of operating income and potential expansion of mNAV from historical ranges of 2.5x-3.0x. Strategy's leveraged exposure to Bitcoin and allowing it to capture substantial increased upside through systematic accumulation and professionally managed treasury strategy.

Potential inclusion in the S&P 500 index is a critical structural catalyst that could generate an almost "automatic" $2-3 billion in passive institutional buying without regard for the performance of Bitcoin. Strategy has now satisfied most of the eligibility criteria following improved operating profitability (due to fair value accounting), and now satisfies the only remaining eligibility criteria related to proven profitability for a sustained period (at least a full fiscal year) and sustained market capitalization greater than $15 billion. Inclusion in the index would also generate systematic demand from passive funds and extend legitimacy of Strategy's approach to Bitcoin treasury.

Strategy's innovative structure of preferred securities creates ongoing catalysts through expanding investor access and diversified funding sources. The successful timing of raising $2.5 billion biannually only with STRC preferred stock, to fund 21,021 purchases of bitcoin, without diluting common stock ownership, shows the growing appetite for Bitcoin-backed securities among yield-focused institutional investors. Other preferred capital offerings can be systematically created to fund continuous Bitcoin accumulation, while attracting new categories of investors looking to gain exposure to cryptocurrencies, with a structured yield component.

Management's 42/42 strategic plan represents a systematic long-term catalyst involving $42 billion in equity and $42 billion in debt financing to acquire $84 billion in Bitcoin over a five-year period to 2027. If Management is able to execute this plan successfully, the long-term capital appreciation of mNAV could continue to build upon premium appreciation already gained, along with an allocation of corporate capital that may ultimately demonstrate true versatility across market conditions for investors to access Strategy's securities, during several market cycles.

Growing corporate bitcoin adoption can have the same potential catalyst to the broader ecosystem we support within our treasury approach. Expanding adoption by corporate entities, improvements in regulatory clarity, and potentially supporting foray by various governments into optional Bitcoin strategic reserves for institutional capital like Strategy would only further validate the strategy of staggering Treasury holding by corporates, while increasing the amount of additional capital being allocated into Bitcoin by several institutional managers.

Risks

The mere fact that Strategy has bitcoin leverage creates material risk vectors that require thoughtful consideration and suitable position sizing for all investors, apart from bitcoin conviction. The price of bitcoin correction represents the foundational risk across all other risk types, since prolong weakness in cryptocurrencies may trigger multiple adverse events to happen concurrently. Based on management stress test analysis, they suggest that strategy will survive considering their $73,288 cost basis, however, significant and prolonged weakness below $60,000 - $70,000 could require liquidation activity or refinancing under not optimal market conditions particularly if bitcoin price exceeds credit market stress and liquidity.

The dilution risk has become more problematic considering that Strategy's Bitcoin positions may exceed $75 billion, which creates deepening challenges to achieve BTC yield targets without perhaps issuing considerable equity. VanEck analysis data provides the economic deterioration impact clearly: for example, one basis point of BTC Yield now requires 58 btc of capital versus 2.6 btc historically which demonstrates how Strategy's forward success creates subsequent execution problems for itself. While the company maintains dilution regime at mNAV especially for yield driven targets, the decision to execute an issuance could occur without regard to price as it relates to mNAV but has made this regime $0 behaviourally as contemplating raising equity below 2.5x bitcoin NAV is still dilution. It is worth stating that the current aggressive targets of 30% annual BTC yields may mean the company will eventually face either the opportunity to raise equity in a value damaging way or simply abandon the yield target altogether.

Refinancing risk is a major consideration, as Strategy has $8.2 billion of convertible debt maturing from 2028-2030 with strike prices of $307-$433. If Bitcoin trades below the conversion prices at maturity, Strategy will need to refinance in potentially unfriendly credit conditions. As demonstrated during the 2022-2023 cryptocurrency winter, Strategy was able access minimal capital, a stark contrast to the $28 billion raised during 2024-2025. Market access can vaporize when access to capital is most urgently needed. This could also represent liquidity traps during prolonged corrections of Bitcoin when both equity and debt markets could be absent.

Premium compression is another ongoing valuation risk over the horizon. Strategy's current (1.84x mNAV) price could move inversely toward a historical low of 1.5x if conditions are negative enough to affect price and demand in the market, which would eliminate the primary equity funding mechanism when capital intensity is highest. Strategy's systematic accumulation strategy depends entirely on maintaining the willingness of investors to pay premiums for Bitcoin exposure - an investor dynamic very well we could potentially see flip in prolonged periods of bear markets in cryptocurrency, or broader corrections across risk-asset classes.

Regulatory uncertainty continues to create tail risks that could drastically shift Strategy's business model, although there are a few recent positive developments. Potential shifts in the treatment of cryptocurrency accounting, taxation of unrealized gains, limits on corporate Bitcoin holdings, or regulatory changes in a key jurisdiction, all represent low-probability, high-impact scenarios that could require significant changes to Strategy. Overall, while the regulatory front appears to be making forward progress, the cryptocurrency ecosystem remains vulnerable to the kind of political and regulatory risk that traditional businesses do not regularly face.

The growing preferred securities ecosystem poses additional complexity and potential conflicts between classes of existing investors that could negatively impact common stockholders. Strategy's STRF trading at a premium in relation to other opportunities, while STRD trades at a discount, is a tangible example of possible reallocations of investor interests in various classes of securities, including preferred equity, which could potentially result in negative impacts for common equity and even potentially impact the mNAV premium which is the ultimate driver of the full accumulation strategy. Management has to find a balance between optimizing complexity in its own capital structure yet not taking focus away from the only thing that creates value for common stockholders.

Conclusion

Strategy has now developed into the most sophisticated institutional Bitcoin leverage available in public equity markets having grown from a defining software company into the world’s largest corporate bitcoin treasury operation. The substantially evolved capital structure, and a systematic accumulation strategy creates one-of-a-kind value for investors looking for heightened cryptocurrency exposure with institutional management and accepted access to capital markets.

Shares provide appreciable upsides around the $229 level and with current trading around 1.84x is reasonable as historical ranges suggest price trading between 1.58x and 2.29x Bitcoin NAV as shown in this chart without any access to Bitcoin NAV (net asset value). If Bitcoin can achieve analyst price targets between $150,000-$200,000, the stock has immense upside, while management's sophisticated capital markets execution of their bitcoin treasury-related "BTC Yield" strategy should outperform direct bitcoin ownership under favorable market conditions. The year-to-date BTC yield of 25%, increased guidance of 30% per year and a successful preferred offerings securities program demonstrate management has the capability to execute complicated bitcoin accumulation strategies at an unmatched scale.

Investors will still have to accept the considerable risks associated with Strategy's business model. The stock is about 2.5 times as volatile as Bitcoin, while having a 0.65 correlation, so significant extremes in any direction will occur. Other considerable risks exist as well, such as possible dilution from aggressive capital-raising strategies, the challenges of refinancing if Bitcoin were to weaken below its convertible strike prices, regulatory uncertainty, and binary outcomes determined solely on cryptocurrency price movements. Strategy is a leveraged Bitcoin derivative more than a diversified equity investment and so requires acceptable position sizing, no matter how much you believe in your investment thesis.

At current valuations, for Bitcoin bulls looking for leveraged exposure with professionally managed treasury, Strategy may offer attractive risk-adjusted returns, particularly for investors who are less comfortable with their own custody of cryptocurrency, or are looking to accumulate Bitcoin systematically beyond their personal purchase limits. More conservative investors, looking for something simpler than Strategy as opposed to more complex aspects of their business, should consider investing in spot ETFs such as BlackRock's IBIT which offer direct tracking of the underlying cryptocurrency, without any execution risk of the corporation, or risks of dilution or convertible debt.

To put it simply, the investment case is a leveraged bet on the rise in the price of bitcoin, with Strategy providing a unique upside with its architecture of capital and its systematic accumulation strategy which is unlike any of its peers. Given the extreme volatility and binary outcomes, sophisticated investors should have no more than a 3-5% Strategy exposure as part of total risk in their portfolio and review it as a form of leveraged cryptocurrency exposure and not as traditional equity diversification.

data.title: SDIV: The ETF That Pays You To Lose Money
data.description:
For income focused investors, portfolio growth is not optional. Emphasizing on yields that are tax friendly and higher payouts as a focus is okay. But where do you fund those payouts from? That is a key problem many high yield or income strategies turn a blind eye to. If there is no growth in the portfolio, the dividends being paid out are nothing but a return of the invested capital.

The Global X SuperDividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SDIV) is one such ETF that promise and delivers the high dividends, but the underlying capital goes through systematic erosion over time. So, while a 10% yield on $100 investment returns $10 initially, within 5 years, the returns taper to $5 even at the same yield because the portfolio value has shrunk to $50. The issue with SDIV is not because it focuses on yield. The issue is the lack of balance between yield and quality picks that make payouts more sustainable in the longer run. I do not see value in the SDIV proposition as a long-term income investor. Tactical uses can be evaluated.

Methodology and Portfolio

SDIV replicates the Solactive Global SuperDividend Index. The methodology of the index is simple. It is a simple equal weighted collection of the top dividend yielding stocks globally. This includes the US and emerging markets. It also allows REITs and MLPs.

The offshoot of the methodology is the obvious high yield which is the primary objective. The TTM dividend yield is now at 9.9%. Monthly dividend payout is also an attractive structure for dividend focused investors. The dividend focus does not bias the portfolio geographically, although sectoral tilts may be expected. Overall, diversification should not be a concern and concentration risks are also mitigated by an equal weighted approach.

There are some constraints on the selection universe that mitigates risks to an extent. There are minimum liquidity and market cap qualification criteria. There is a minimum yield criteria of 6% for a stock to qualify because of the ETFs objective, which is very high in itself. There is also a high cut off at 20% to prevent riskier bets from seeping in. The selection process also looks at any announcements or outlook changes to the dividend to proactively screen inclusions.

The screens are not sufficient to ensure quality holdings for the portfolio. A 20% yield for cut-off does not serve its purpose in trimming riskier positions. A quarterly rebalance may still be acceptable if the underlying shares are financially strong. Most importantly, there is no filter at all to ensure some quality check is in place for the portfolio. Even a market cap selection criteria often works as a screen - here its role is limited to qualification only. And there are no other fundamental filters as well.

The resultant portfolio is as expected of the methodology. The concentration in the US is healthy at less than 25%. Among other markets, there is no bias between developed or emerging economies. I see a healthy presence of Brazil, China and Britain - showing a decent allocation in developed as well as emerging economies.

The sectoral allocation does have a heavy concentration in energy followed by mortgage REITs and financials. Even REITs are have decent weighting. The concentration on REITs, mortgage REITs and real estate makes the portfolio heavily dependent on the economic cycles. Energy allocations make the portfolio more volatile and, if not layered with quality factors often speculative. A true dividend portfolio, even if simply chasing yields should have a heavier concentration in financials where the risk is much reduced overall and long-term growth is a possibility.

I will refrain from posting the top 10 holdings and commenting on that because this is an equal weighted portfolio with changes in allocations due to share price changes between rebalances. You can take a look at all the holdings here. I did and saw some currency positions to hedge against currency risks. Among stocks, my subjective judgement saw only 10-15% exposure in names I would call quality with a yield focus - names like say Parex, Guess?, OPAP, Phoenix and Vale, among a few others. SDIV's focus is naturally tilted towards small and mid caps compared to the large cap concentration in most other yield ETFs, even the international ones. The holdings naturally show significant exposure to high leveraged financials, high payout cyclicals and even distressed or special situation names. SDIV is almost a maverick among yield focused plays, even including the international ones like VYMI.

Performance Drags

Empirical performance data manifests all the concerns the methodology points to. The high-risk high-yield approach could have worked out better in at least a few eras, but even a few years of consistent total return trends are missing. In fact, the total return numbers make the Sell case for SDIV even worse. I have compared SDIV's total returns against a random basket of yield ETFs for perspective. SPYD is completely US focused and quite conservative given that it starts with a large cap universe already filtered by the broader S&P 500 methodology. HDV (again US focused) is the most conservative in that it has a high-quality screen for its yield focus. VYMI is international, but has a larger portfolio and also screens for dividend sustainability. VDIV is also a global exposure fund with an eye on dividend history and stability. The methodology (reflected in total returns) of all the yield focused ETFs has its own strengths and pitfalls, but does have a healthy total return in the long term. SDIV is flat with barely a few years of total return growth that has never sustained.

The yield focus is clearly hurting the portfolio for SDIV. It goes to show how a balanced yield is often the best way to sustain dividends in the long term. The cost for higher yield pays off negatively and by a margin beyond a point. SDIV's NAV has almost consistently eroded with brief periods of stability. Yields have consistently remained high, and in fact move in negative correlation to the capital investment, as expected.

The expense ratio of 0.58% is not concerning, but its higher expense ratio to VYMI at 0.17%, HDV at 0.08%, SPYD at 0.08% or even WDIV at 0.4%, is far higher for no apparent advantageous positioning.

No Case for SDIV

A volatile risky growth stocks is still okay to ride, if I have researched it fundamentally and know my entry exit triggers. A volatile risky portfolio that is passive and not in my control is difficult to Buy. Historical performance adds to the overall Sell thesis. For passive ETFs, a double digit yield is often unsustainable. For DIY approaches, that can still be attempted, balancing quality alongside yield considerations and staying agile on execution. SDIV doesn't just underperform other yield alternatives (lower yields but sustainable), it returns close to nothing over the long term.

Even for tactical use, I cannot recommend SDIV because it doesn't follow a theme that can be predicted to be rewarding in some market conditions. At most, one can count on risk-on rallies, lower interest growth regimes where broader markets remain upbeat. That is not the current regime surely. To SDIV's defense, the total returns have been relatively better of in the past 2-3 years. But don't rely on chances. SDIV's methodology looks very speculative and have historically seen eventual erosion even after brief periods of relative, just about acceptable resilience.

data.title: Royal Gold: A Gold Royalty Giant Going Through Massive Acquisitions, But Lagging Peers
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Royal Gold (NASDAQ:RGLD) is one of the largest royalty and streaming companies, with a focus predominantly on gold, silver and especially copper, which has been going through significant acquisitions in recent months. Right now, the company seems poised for significant growth ahead, while the stock has been lagging behind most of its peers, being up ~30% YTD compared to ~50% to 70% recorded by their main peers, which is in line with gold’s ~28.4% return. However, as streamers offer a middle ground when it comes to leverage on gold (miners being the higher tier, with more risk and reward), and given the company’s recent acquisitions that I’ll mention below, this seems like an unjustified response from the market.

On top of this, the company is quite attractive from a geographical diversification point of view, with ~28% of NAV in Canada and ~13% in the US prior to a deal that will add exposure to Zambia as well that I’ll mention later, being one of the best portfolio concentrations from a jurisdiction risk point of view compared to peers. Back in 2024, around 60% of their revenue came from Canada, USA and Australia, which are considered to be among the highest-quality jurisdictions for mining.

As they say, they are large enough to grow and compete in high-stakes deals, but also small enough to still have room to grow significantly as their investments pay off. Given the gold equivalent ounces guidance of 330,000 to 375,000 expected in 2025 in the image above (which they maintained following the latest earnings report; which will be higher following a recent deal that should add 35,000 to 40,000 ounces per year), we’re looking at a ~$11.34 billion market cap, while competitors who expect to produce less than twice this much come at almost 3 to 4 times higher market caps.

As you can expect, their Q2 report came with records for pretty much everything, from the revenue of $209.6 million, operating cash flow of $152.8 million and the earnings of $132.3 million. Their free cash flow hasn’t really been amazing for this market cap, but we can see an overall solid one over the past decade, with significant drops when the company announces massive CAPEX used for deals - and it’s overall in line with peers.

In this case, the net income might offer the clearer picture, showing how the company has more than quadrupled from the levels seen back in 2017 and 2019, on the back of the jump in gold/silver prices and growth in their attributable production, which peaked a couple of years ago but is now poised for a rebound, as mentioned below.

Financially, based on their latest report, we see an excellent position (similar to most streamers), with the current assets covering the total liabilities and zero debt. However, we’re looking at ~$3.32 billion in equity on a $11.34 billion market cap, and although the balance sheet is backward-looking when it comes to the value of the assets (the NAV shows their current value), we’re still talking about a high price to pay for their net assets. This is normal when gold is trading near its highest levels in history, but could represent a risk if prices fall. However, that’s for a normal company, not a gold streamer. The business model is very solid and helps the company return a profit almost no matter the environment, and the lack of debt means there’s no need for them to sell in desperation if the environment deteriorates - unlike some miners.

However, as I’ll mention below, the company will access an $875 million credit facility, which they will likely repay in the near future as they did before with other debt cycles. As you can see in the image above, this is far from their first draw, and I like that they focus on repaying rather than letting it mount up to unsustainable levels, which adds to the safety of this business model.

Dividends & Buybacks

Similar to peers, their dividend yield is far from good, especially after the stock went up. As they are generally paying the equivalent of a ~20% payout ratio, this will very likely increase as a result of the significant jumps we’ve seen this year. This would mark their 25th annual dividend increase, but since they aren’t an S&P 500 component, they are technically not going to be a Dividend Aristocrat, although they’re part of the broader S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index (only requires 20 years of dividend increases)

Despite a ~15% dividend CAGR from 2000 to 2025, the company still pays a yield below inflation, but again, that’s because streamers are a middle ground between gold itself and miners. They come with some leverage on gold plus a dividend (in this case, below inflation), while gold doesn’t pay anything. Streamers, on the other hand, are businesses that can compound thanks to their investments, and buying this back a decade ago would result in ~3% yields on cost, as well as a 3x return, although we’re talking about the lowest and highest points for the commodity, so it’s not necessarily a fair comparison for the long-term.

Risks & Opportunities

In my Sandstorm Gold (SAND) analysis published recently, I mentioned how the company’s plans to double their attributable gold production by 2030 combined with a low valuation compared to peers despite doubling over the past year make it a Buy.

Less than a week later, Royal Gold made the, in my opinion, wise decision of buying them together with Horizon Copper (OTCQX:HNCUF) - which was ~34% owned by Sandstorm - in a deal valued at more than $3.5 billion in total. This adds very significant growth through gold and copper exposure from a diversified set of countries to Royal Gold, and I think this might be one of the best mergers/acquisitions we’ve seen this year (so far) and it’s quite tough to beat.

Upon closing, the existing RGLD and SAND shareholders will own ~77% and ~23%, of RGLD’s issued and outstanding shares on a fully diluted basis, which is seen as an issue in the market, hence the ~5.6% decline on the announcement. Still, looking at it from a long-term perspective, we’re talking about a company with a massive growth pipeline which was bought before it could really show what it can truly do.

Yes, this comes with dilution and the market wasn’t very happy about it, but we have to remember that when you buy cashflow-producing assets, the per share results can even be accretive, depending on how good the deal was. In Sandstorm’s case, we’re talking about a royalty company that’s expecting to double its attributable production by 2030, which is well above what Wheaton (WPM) and Franco-Nevada (FNV) expect - both of which I recently analyzed.

As RGLD says, this deal is even accretive to the Net Asset Value for the company, estimating a 44% increase in NAV from this deal, as Sandstorm was trading at a low P/NAV. So, this dilution results in a massive increase in NAV, as well as an immediate ~26% increase in 2025’s production (without even including Horizon), plus Sandstorm’s pipeline to double it by 2030, which is an amazing deal and quite a steal for Sandstorm shareholders, although they’ll get shares in the combined company and can still enjoy its future.

Shortly after announcing this deal, they also announced acquiring a gold stream on First Quantum’s flagship Kansanshi copper-gold mine in Zambia for $1 billion, paying from 20% to 35% of the spot gold price for each ounce delivered, depending on certain conditions described in the press release. This could be somewhat risky, as $1 billion is very significant for a now $11.34 billion market cap due to the country they gain exposure to and the fact that $875 million of that will be funded through a credit facility and $125 million through cash, but the overall business remains one of the most Americas and gold focused streamers. And as a note, these two were not the only deals RGLD got into this year.

With streamers, I believe their ability to issue stock and debt is one of their strongest points. As the companies don’t make a lot of cash for it to lead to significant investments, these external sources of capital can offer the key to their growth, as we can see with deals such as the ones above. Unlike miners, who sometimes trade at some of their lowest points depending on the commodity cycle, streamers often come at very high ratios thanks to their higher stability.

As for the long-term, I remain bullish both on gold and especially copper, and I believe their diversification into copper is actually a very good move. When it comes to gold, I believe this commodity’s potential is nearly unlimited, as it is not tied to “real life” sources of demand that much compared to any other metal, including precious ones. We can see that almost half of the demand for gold comes from investment and central banks, while a majority comes from jewelry, none of these being the most rational or elastic sources of demand. In short, higher gold prices wouldn’t make investors or central banks want to buy less or diminish jewelry demand significantly, and they could even lead to an increase in demand especially if we get into a fear of missing out or hype scenario. If you think about it, with all the tariffs, rate cuts devaluing the dollar, uncertainty, wars, etc., the fact that precious metals went up so much this year shouldn’t be a surprise.

To that, add the fact that the median AISC for gold has surpassed $1,500 in Q1 2025, which is more than 50% higher than Q4 2020. This means that more than half of the gold produced is now coming at an AISC above $1,500 per ounce, which is very significant for the future prices of the metal, as a drop to that level - or even $2,000 or more - could make a huge chunk of the production unsustainable, meaning mines closing, bankruptcies, production cuts, etc. Back in 2016-2018, it’s no surprise that we were seeing gold at a bit over $1,000 or $1,200 per ounce, as that level was enough for a wide majority of mines to produce gold at a profit. Now however, that level would imply that about 80-85% of the production wouldn’t be profitable, which is nowhere near a sustainable level, theoretically causing a massive drop in supply.

As I mentioned in the recent Teck Resources (TECK) stock analysis, copper on the other hand is much more tied to the economy - especially China, which accounts for over 50% of total copper demand! Today, a majority of copper demand comes from Electrical and Electronics (~35-40%), Construction and Infrastructure (25-30%), Transportation (~15%) and other uses. This is a main reason why it’s also called “doctor copper” because it’s usually a very good representation of the economy, as demand for copper depends on the state of the global economy. But, the main thing about copper is that the demand is expected to grow significantly thanks to clean energy transition and storage, EVs and charging, data centers, etc., while production is expected to struggle to keep up.

However, since China represents a significant source of demand and an even higher refiner, we can see this leading to potential issues caused by tariffs - if they continue - so it’s kind of a double-edged sword, and we’ve seen copper prices dropping by a historic ~22% in a single day back at the end of July when Trump “eased” copper tariffs. This relationship with the economy means that on the one hand, good times can be very beneficial for the demand and price, while the bad ones can lead to very significant crashes, as we’ve seen many times before.

As a final point here, I believe it’s important to keep in mind that these assets are long-term investments. What this stock does is basically offer exposure to some of the best mines on the planet, but that too is something that shows results over time. Especially in Sandstorm’s case, this deal will likely be very accretive especially over the next half a decade, when their projects and deals begin to show the effects of significant ramp-ups, but time and patience remain very important with such an investment. However, in this environment, RGLD might be quite an opportunity now after buying Sandstorm at an attractive valuation, and their exposure to copper may be something that will help a lot over the next decade.

Peer Comparison

Compared to their main peers, Franco-Nevada (FNV), Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), OR Royalties (OR) and Triple Flag Precious Metals (TFPM), we actually see the best valuation metrics in pretty much every regard. I also included Sandstorm Gold (SAND), to show how they will consolidate their level at some of the lowest ratios even after this transaction, plus the very good growth potential the deal comes with.

Valuation

From the midpoint of ~352,500 GEO expected this year, if we add the difference from First Quantum to get to the ~35,000 - 40,000 GEO they expect, as well as ~75,000 increase from Sandstorm, we’re looking at over 450,000 GEO by 2030 just from these deals alone, without accounting for any growth in their own projects before these acquisitions. Rounding it to 500,000 to assume a less than 3% CAGR for their other projects, we’re looking at an increase of ~42.85% in their estimated GEO compared to 2025, and about 66.5% higher compared to 2024, which is in line with their peers.

As a result of ~40% higher gold prices this year compared to 2024’s averages and the potential to see solid prices throughout the long-term, we could easily be looking at a company that’s making even more than $730 million in free cash flows by 2030, although it ultimately depends on the price of gold.

I will assume a starting FCF of $317.7 million in 2024, a CAGR that brings the FCF to $730 million in 2029 (~18.06%), a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years as their pipeline would naturally fall and they’d have to find new projects, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a WACC of 7% (assumes: 7.15% cost of equity, 4.3% cost of debt, ~17.5% effective tax rate; takes into account the $825 million credit facility). This way, we get to an enterprise value of approximately $12.55 billion, and when subtracting the $727 million in net debt (following the financing of the First Quantum deal through cash and debt), this returns an equity value of roughly $11.82 billion. With 85 million shares outstanding (following the Sandstorm Gold deal), this gives an intrinsic value of about $139.06 per share, which is ~17.5% below the current price, so the price reflects all these potential achievements quite well.

Conclusions

Overall, I rate Royal Gold a Hold now, as the stock is likely pricing in their significant deals and the price of gold, with most of the potential upside above this estimate coming from better gold prices and deals in the future. The company remains valued quite well compared to their peers however, and a recovery in that to the industry averages could also offer some potential, although it doesn’t seem that significant to justify a buy with a good margin of safety. If anything, these recent acquisitions got them in line with their peers in terms of growth potential. Still, now with Sandstorm being taken out of the equation, they remain one of the better royalty and streaming companies in the market, so definitely something to keep an eye on for potential dips.

Performance figures assume that all distributions are reinvested. For the most recent month-end performance, visit jhinvestments.com.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower- quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms — also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. On the positive side, the fund benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight in emerging-market bonds.

Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.
